title: Blood on Cotton
stainChemistry: Blood is a protein stain — hemoglobin, fibrin, albumin. Cotton is chemically durable: it tolerates cold and warm water, enzymes, peroxide, and (on white cotton) even chlorine bleach. The clock matters: the first 15–30 minutes while the protein is still soluble is when blood comes out easiest. After that, hemoglobin oxidizes to ferric iron-porphyrin and browns into cellulose, and the stain gets progressively harder. Heat at any stage cross-links the protein into the fiber permanently.
whyThisWorks: Cold water halts protein setting and flushes soluble hemoglobin out before it can bind. Protein Spotter (enzymatic digestant, liquid) then breaks peptide bonds on residual stain — cotton tolerates protease enzymes safely, unlike wool or silk. 3% hydrogen peroxide oxidizes residual iron-porphyrin pigment to colorless products. NSD flushes out all chemistry residue before any heat-drying, which would otherwise re-set anything left behind. The sequence stays cold through spotting — heat is the one thing that will permanently set blood on any fiber.
defaultAssumption: Assume the stain is fresh unless the user says otherwise. Start conservative — cold water discipline, enzymatic spotter, peroxide only if needed.

spottingProtocol.technique: Flush from the back, blot hard
spottingProtocol.equipment: Spray bottle, white cotton terry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Work from the back of the garment first. Spray cold water and blot hard from underneath — hydraulic pressure pushes soluble hemoglobin back out the way it came in, rather than forward through the weave. Flip to the front and continue flooding and blotting. Blood should visibly lighten in the water. Keep working until blotting produces clear or pale pink water. Speed matters — protein sets fastest in the first 15–30 minutes. Cold only — any warmth at this stage cooks the protein into the cotton permanently.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B / protease-based enzymatic digestant)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply liquid Protein Spotter directly to the stained area. PRENETT B or any protease-based enzymatic digestant is the pro canonical — a clear liquid, not a powder. Tamp gently with a soft bristle brush. The protease hydrolyzes peptide bonds on residual protein that cold water could not lift. White cotton handles warm enzyme work safely; if using a warm-water enzyme pre-soak for an older stain, 30–40°C is the ceiling. If all you have is a powder pre-soak (Biz, OxiClean, Zout), dissolve it in cold water per the label and apply as a liquid — never sprinkle powder directly on fabric.

For any remaining pink or brown shadow: apply 3% hydrogen peroxide drop-by-drop directly onto the stain. Watch it foam — that is the oxidation reaction lifting residual iron-porphyrin pigment. Flush the moment the shadow disappears. Test an inside seam first on dyed cotton — some reactive dyes lift under peroxide.

Flush thoroughly with NSD to remove all chemistry residue — enzymes, peroxide, and any alkali. Work from the back. Blot; do not rub. All oxidizer and enzyme residue must be fully out before drying, or residual chemistry damages the fiber in storage.

Rinse under running cold water until the water runs clear. Then launder in warm water (cotton tolerates warm once the stain is gone) with regular detergent. Incomplete rinsing leaves peroxide or enzyme residue that yellows the fiber over time. Inspect dry, in daylight, before any hot-dryer pass.

steps: 1. Cold water flush from the back of the fabric. Blot hard, lift out hemoglobin before it sets.
2. Apply liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B or protease-based enzymatic digestant). Tamp. Dwell 5–15 min.
3. If shadow remains: 3% hydrogen peroxide drop-by-drop. Watch the foam. Test inside seam on dyed cotton.
4. NSD flush. Remove all chemistry residue before any heat.
5. Cool rinse, then warm-water wash with standard detergent. Air dry and inspect in daylight before hot-drying.
products: Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B or equivalent protease-based digestant)
Cold running water, standard warm-water laundry detergent
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold through spotting; warm only after stain is fully gone
warnings: Never use hot water or heat on blood — sets protein permanently
Never sprinkle enzyme powder directly on fabric — always dissolve in cold water first
Test 3% hydrogen peroxide on an inside seam on dyed cotton — some reactive dyes lift
Never dry with residual peroxide or enzyme — yellows or degrades the fiber over time

homeSolutions.instruction: Fresh blood on cotton is beatable if you get to it in the first 15–30 minutes. Cold water only. Hot water cooks the protein into the fiber and sets the stain permanently — this is the single most common reason blood stains "never come out."
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never use hot water, a warm dryer, or an iron on a blood stain that's still visible.

homeSolutions.instruction: Hold the fabric under cold running water with the BACK of the stain facing the tap. The water pushes the blood back out the way it came in, rather than through the fiber. Keep going until the water running off runs clear or only faintly pink. This step alone clears most of a fresh stain.

homeSolutions.agent: Lift Dried Residue with a Spoon
homeSolutions.instruction: If there's any dried crust on the surface, lay the fabric stain-side down on a hard surface (counter, glass) and gently scrape off the residue with the edge of a spoon or a butter knife. Work from the outside in so you don't smear. Don't pull hard — you want to lift the crust, not stretch the weave.

Apply an enzyme laundry pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout) per the label — always dissolve powder in cold water first, never sprinkle dry powder on fabric. Work it gently into the stain and let it sit 15 minutes. The enzymes digest residual protein the water rinse couldn't lift.
Put a drop of clear Dawn dish soap on the spot. Work it in gently with a fingertip or a soft toothbrush — no hard scrubbing, which frays cotton fibers. Rinse cool.

If a faint brown or pink shadow remains, dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide on the spot. Watch closely — you'll often see the stain lift in under a minute as the peroxide oxidizes the residual pigment. Rinse with cold water immediately once the shadow is gone. Test on an inside seam first if the cotton is dyed.

Lay flat or hang to air-dry. Inspect the dried fabric under daylight — not the laundry-room bulb — before tumble-drying. Any shadow you can still see will lock in permanently under dryer heat. If there's any doubt, repeat steps 4–6 before drying hot.

If the blood has already been through a hot dryer, is older than 24–48 hours, or survives two full home rounds, take it to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Blood on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], treated at home with cold water, enzyme pretreat, and hydrogen peroxide. No heat applied." Pros have liquid Protein Spotters and controlled oxidation that go further than drugstore peroxide without damaging the fiber.

steps: 1. Flush with cold water from the back of the fabric until the water runs mostly clear.
2. Scrape off any dried crust with the edge of a spoon or butter knife.
3. Apply an enzyme laundry pretreat dissolved in cold water. Let sit 15 minutes.
4. Work a drop of Dawn into the spot gently. Rinse cool.
5. For remaining shadow: dab 3% hydrogen peroxide, rinse as soon as it lifts. Test inside seam first on dyed cotton.
6. Air dry. Inspect in daylight before any hot dryer — residual stain sets permanently in dryer heat.
products: Enzyme laundry pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after two full rounds. Professional dry cleaners have liquid Protein Spotters and controlled oxidation that go further than home peroxide without damaging cotton.
warnings: Never use hot water — heat sets protein permanently into cotton
Never sprinkle enzyme powder directly on fabric — dissolve in cold water first
Never rub hard — frays cotton fiber and can smear the stain
Test hydrogen peroxide on an inside seam on dyed cotton

neverDo: Never use hot water, warm dryer, or iron on blood — heat sets protein permanently
Never sprinkle enzyme powder directly on fabric — always dissolve in cold water first
Never dry with residual peroxide or enzyme — yellows or degrades fiber in storage
fiberSensitivities: White cotton tolerates enzymes, peroxide, and chlorine bleach (colored cotton: no bleach)
Colored cotton: test peroxide on inside seam first; avoid chlorine entirely
Heat-sensitive only in the sense that heat sets the stain — cotton itself tolerates high laundry temps once the stain is gone

materialWarnings: No hot water — blood protein sets permanently on any fiber if heated
No chlorine bleach on colored cotton — strips dyes
Test 3% hydrogen peroxide on an inside seam first if the cotton is dyed
Always dissolve enzyme powders in cold water first — never apply dry

escalation.when: If stain persists after H₂O₂ and enzyme pretreat, if the blood was heat-set by a dryer or iron, if the stain is older than 24–48 hours, or if two home rounds don't improve it — take it to a dry cleaner.
escalation.whatToTell: Tell them: "Blood on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], treated at home with cold water, enzyme pretreat, and 3% hydrogen peroxide. No heat applied." Give the age and prior treatment so they don't duplicate failed approaches.

contextQuestions: Has the fabric been through heat — dryer, iron, warm water?
Is the cotton white or dyed?
What's the garment — shirt / sheet / carpet backing / other?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Fresh + no heat = high success rate. Heat-set or aged stains often need a pro. Dyed cotton requires peroxide testing on an inside seam. White cotton gives the widest chemistry latitude of any common fabric.
